content:
We've recently been trying to solve an issue causing a deadlock on ::unbind() in Tyr, and after further investigation, the root cause seems to be nested Devres (an object wrapped into a Devres that also have Devres fields).

My understanding of the situation is that devres_release_all() moves all resources to a temporary list that's iterated to release each resource in reverse creation order. If a root devres has its T::drop() function called, the nested devres will be dropped too, and we're going to hit that path because the resource list has been emptied earlier in devres_release_all(). So we're going to wait on the devm completion, which will never happen because this item is still in the temporary and will only be destroyed later on when release_nodes reaches this item.

Dunno if it's a bug, or if nested Devres are prohibited, but if it's the latter, then we should probably document it and find a way to avoid people shooting themselves in the foot.
cc @Danilo Krummrich @Alice Ryhl ^
Can you please share the code where the problem occurs?
This is the workaround we have at the moment => https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/panfrost/linux/-/merge_requests/56/diffs#dbdd2c5024317f2c80128c91a823e224b3a41550_96_114
root Devres comes from https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/dbrouwer/linux/-/blob/dbrouwer/fw-boot/drivers/gpu/drm/tyr/driver.rs#L169 (Registration object is bound to the device as a devres)
that's one of the nested Devres => https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/dbrouwer/linux/-/blob/dbrouwer/fw-boot/drivers/gpu/drm/tyr/mmu/address_space.rs#L74
there's another one here => https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/dbrouwer/linux/-/blob/dbrouwer/fw-boot/drivers/gpu/drm/tyr/mmu/address_space.rs#L94
Thanks, do you have the commit sha of the workaround? (I'd assume that SHA^ would then be a commit where things are broken).
I'll take a look then after lunch.
Note that this lives in a downstream branch, so the sha is not super relevant, but here is the commit introducing the workaround aac949032a0a ("driver.rs fw")
the branch is here https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/dbrouwer/linux/-/blob/dbrouwer/fw-boot
/cc @Deborah Brouwer
It's interesting because it also tells me the base you branched off of.

It's likely that there's nothing working before/after that commit, because this patchset has not been cleaned up for submission yet, so my point about commit being not super relevant was more a "don't try to bisect" disclaimer
I'm sure we can have a simple reproducer based on samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs, if that helps
Oh yeah, I don't have hardware anyways, so I can't bisect this either way.
But I already have a suspicion what's going on, so I just want to make sure I don't base conclusions on assumptions. :)

@Boris Brezillon Your understanding is correct, there is indeed nesting going on, but indirectly. When the drm::Registration is revoked you likely also drop the last reference of the drm::Device, which calls the destructor of its private data which holds the Devres<IoMem>. Which is a perfectly valid thing to do.
Technically, this specific issue will go away with some changes I currently work on.
But I think it would be good to have a separate fix for this -- will dig in.
Thanks for the report!

But I think it would be good to have a separate fix for this -- will dig in.

OOC, what's your plan to fix the issue?

The answer is a bit longer.

The big hammer is an inner reference count, which I had in the past before getting rid of it for optimization purposes. (There is nothing wrong with the reference count, probe() and remove() are not hot paths at all and a separate allocation for a device resource does not hurt.)

However, with the rework I'm working on the specific issue with registrations having a reference count of something else that owns a Devres goes away. The only issue being left would be if you do Devres<Devres<T>>etc. which makes no sense. In fact, Devres is not a mechanism for drivers to model lifetimes. The only valid pattern is Devres<DEVICE_RESOURCE>, which effectively makes direct nesting invalid.

Therefore, once the rework is done, we would not need an inner reference count, but pay the price that Devres::new becomes unsafe, which I think is OK, since it should - with maybe a few minor exceptions - only be constructed by core code anyways.
This topic was moved here from #DRM > Deadlock caused by nested Devres by Gary Guo.
Danilo Krummrich has marked this topic as resolved.
